title: Woodpecker
alt: If you don't have an extension cord I can get that too.  Because we're friends!  Right?
transcript:
[The first sixteen panels are set in four rows with each row having four equal-sized panels.]
[Megan and Beret Guy are standing on a deck.]
Beret Guy: A woodpecker!
pop pop pop
Megan: Yup.

[Woodpecker knocking against a tree.]
pop pop pop pop
Megan (off-panel): He hatched about this time last year.

[Megan walks out of frame to the left.]

[Beret Guy stands on the deck alone.]
Beret Guy: ...Woodpecker?
Beret Guy...It's your birthday!

[Beret Guy continues to stand on the deck alone.]
Beret Guy: Did you know?

[Beret Guy continues to stand on the deck alone.]
Beret Guy: Did... did nobody tell you?

[Beret Guy continues to stand on the deck alone.]

[Beret Guy walks to the left.]

[The tree base.]

[Beret Guy brings a gift (a box with a ribbon on top) toward the tree.]

[The gift is at the bottom of the tree while Beret Guy looks up.]

[Beret Guy walks away from the tree to the left.]

[The gift is at the base of the tree.]

[The woodpecker stands on the upper part of the base of the tree, looking down at the gift.]

[The woodpecker is perched atop the gift.]

[The woodpecker pulls at the gift's ribbon with its beak.]

[The last panel is as wide as the earlier rows of four panels.]
[The woodpecker flies away, with clouds at the left and right of the panel, holding onto the cord of a power drill.]
explanation:
Beret Guy observes a . A woodpecker is a type of bird known for using its bill to bore holes into trees to get access to and eat the insects living inside. Megan notes that the bird hatched approximately a year ago. Beret Guy seems touched by this fact, and attempts to explain to the woodpecker that it is the woodpecker's birthday.

Beret Guy leaves a present by the foot of the tree where the woodpecker is nesting. The woodpecker opens the present which turns out to be a power drill, a mechanical tool that is used to bore holes into wood, much quicker than a bird normally could. Not that this would be of much use to the bird, as the bird has no electricity and so no means of powering the drill, let alone  with which to operate the drill. In spite of this, the woodpecker has not only unwrapped the present; it has apparently accepted it, and flies off with it. Also, remember that this is Beret Guy who gave the woodpecker the drill: he may have given it some other means of working without electricity.

The title text appears to be Beret Guy talking to the bird still, pointing out that he can get the bird an extension cord to operate the drill because, in his mind, they are friends. In actuality, the bird may or may not care about the drill or Beret Guy. An extension cord, also, would not do much good as the bird would still have no electricity, and if it did have a source of electricity and was able to use the drill, it wouldn't be able to fly further than the length of the extension cord, which isn't far for a bird. This could be a reference to 509: Induced Current.
